Notice Of Motion And Motion For Protective Order To Reduce The List Of Defendants Retained Expert Witnesses For Trial
Off Calendar Oct-01-2025 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: PLAINTIFF HILARY MCKEAN's Motion For Protective Order To Reduce The List Of Defendants Retained Expert Witnesses For Trial.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff Hilary McKean's motion for a protective order to reduce the list of defendants' retained expert witnesses for trial is denied.

Defendant Mark Hanson has disclosed four expert witnesses who are at issue on this motion, Kim Norman, M.D.; Mitchell Eisen, Ph.D.; Jennifer Tomasic, MSc Psych; and Paul Fitzgerald, M.D. McKean contends their testimony substantially overlaps, all of the testimony each will give could be presented by the doctor who conducted the examination of McKean, and Hanson therefore should be restricted to one expert.

Code of Civil Procedure, section 2034.250, provides in relevant part:

"(b) The court, for good cause shown, may make any order that justice requires to protect any party from unwarranted annoyance, embarrassment, oppression, or undue burden and expense. The protective order may include, but is not limited to, one or more of the following directions:
. . .
"(6) That a party or a side reduce the list of employed or retained experts designated by that party or side under subdivision (b) of Section 2034.210."

The court has reviewed Hanson's expert designations and concludes that they are not unduly cumulative. While they are all on topics that relate in some way to the reliability of McKean's recollections, they offer different opinions. Nor is the court persuaded by McKean's argument that, to the extent the opinions are different, they could all be offered by one mental health expert that Hanson could call in lieu of several. The experts have different backgrounds and areas of focus, which may bear on the weight a jury gives their testimony. "Identical or virtually identical evidence may not be cumulative if there is significance to the evidentiary weight to be given." (Monroy v. City of Los Angeles (2008) 164 Cal.App.4th 248, 267.) The court concludes no protective order is warranted. This order is without prejudice to the trial court's in limine rulings.

Order submitted.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (301/CVA)

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Seal Re: Unredacted Declaration Of Jennifer Fiore Lodged On May 22, 2025
Off Calendar Jul-16-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances:
Atty. Jennifer Fiore, Esq. (via Zoom videoconference) of Fiore Achermann (tel: 415-550-0650) for the Plaintiff.
Atty. Gerald Kipper, Esq. of Murphy, Pearson, Bradley & Feeney (tel: 415-788-1900) for the Defendant.

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT MARK HANSON's Motion To Seal Re: Unredacted Declaration Of Jennifer Fiore Lodged On May 22, 2025.

Matter argued and submitted. Upon conclusion, the Court adopts its tentative ruling.

The motion is granted as to exhibits 2 [discovery letter] and 5 [Hanson deposition] that are attached to the Fiore Declaration filed on May 22, 2025, in opposition to defendant's motion to continue the trial.

CRC 2.550(a)(3) provides: "These [sealing] rules do not apply to discovery motions and records filed or lodged in connection with discovery motions or proceedings. However, the rules do apply to discovery materials that are used at trial or submitted as a basis for adjudication of matters other than discovery motions or proceedings." Here, the adduced exhibits are discovery records and they were not "filed in connection with a motion on a substantive issue" in the case. (Mercury Interactive Corp. v. Klein (2007) 158 Cal.App.4th 60, 100.) The court therefore seals those exhibits.

The motion is denied as to the Fiore declaration itself. To seal that document, the court must find: (1) There exists an overriding interest that overcomes the right of public access to the record; (2) The overriding interest supports sealing the record; (3) A substantial probability exists that the overriding interest will be prejudiced if the record is not sealed; (4) The proposed sealing is narrowly tailored; and (5) No less restrictive means exist to achieve the overriding interest. (CRC 2.550(d).)

Here, defendant does not convincingly demonstrate that sealing the declaration is necessary to preserve his right to a fair trial. A robust voir dire and jury instructions will prevent any alleged prejudice to defendant. Defendant also fails show that his right to privacy overcomes the right of public access.

Order submitted.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Court reporter: Judi Johnson, CSR# 14174 (tel: 516-965-6565 / email: oficialreporter@pm.me) (301/CVA)

Off Calendar Jul-08-2024 Sustained
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES:
Atty. JENNIFER FIORE, Esq. of Fiore Acherman (Tel: 415-550-0650) for plaintiff Hilary McKean (via Zoom videoconference;
Atty. GERALD C. KIPPER, Esq. of Murphy, Pearson, Bradley & Feeney (Tel: 415-788-1900) for defendant Mark Hanson.

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT MARK HANSON'S DEMURRER TO FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant's demurrer to the first amended complaint (FAC) is sustained with leave to amend.
Plaintiff pleads that defendant (her brother) sexually abused her in the 1970s when both were children. Defendant demurs to four counts of the nine-count FAC.

1: "Childhood Sexual Abuse (CCP 340.1)." The "Legislature has impliedly recognized a right to a civil action for 'childhood sexual abuse'," but that action is not found in CCP 340.1, which instead enacted "statutes of limitations for such actions." (Angie M. v. Sup. Ct. (1995) 37 Cal.App.4th 1217, 1225.)

2: "Sexual Battery (Cal. Civ. Code 1708.5)." Civil Code 1708.5 was enacted after the alleged abuse occurred. Plaintiff effectively concedes this point, but suggests she could plead "sexual battery" as "a common law cause of action." (Opp. 2:19.)

4: "Gender Violence." "Plaintiff agrees that the statutes underlying this" count "were enacted after the abuse occurred" so she will dismiss it. (Opp. 2:24-3:2.)

7: "Bane Act Violation (Civ. Code 52.1)." "Plaintiff agrees that the statutes underlying this" count "were enacted after the abuse occurred" so she will dismiss it. (Opp. 2:24-3:2.)

Order signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr.; Clerk: S. Kane; Not Reported. (302/RBU)
